The biggest financial win so far for $MELI with AI is in customer service. In the past, handling a full customer support case cost the company about a dollar per interaction. To lower this expense, they built an internal AI assistant. In the final months of 2025, this assistant managed over nine million customer chats. The AI successfully fixed nearly 90 percent of these problems without any human help. This massive success saves MELI immense amounts of time and money while keeping operations smooth. 

MercadoLibre is also using AI to directly generate consumer activity. In late 2025, they released a new AI assistant for Mercado Pago. This tool is a highly capable financial agent. Users can talk or type to the app to execute real financial tasks. They can ask the AI to pay bills, send money to friends, or check their spending habits. This makes banking very easy for users. It removes friction and keeps people engaging with the app more often. 

However, they remain very honest about the hard parts of scaling AI. They gave their software engineers new AI coding tools to speed up development. But leadership noticed that writing code faster does not instantly make the whole engineering job faster. Programmers still have to go to meetings, plan features, and fix broken code. 

Running all of this AI is incredibly expensive. Computer chips are hard to find globally, and cloud computing costs a massive amount of money. Their technology infrastructure bills are steadily going up.

They continue to prepare for a major strategic threat on the horizon. Younger people might eventually start using chat tools to buy things instead of opening shopping apps. MercadoLibre knows this shift could happen and is preparing for it. To survive, they are adopting a strategy to work with OpenAI while also fighting against them. They are a huge enterprise customer of OpenAI and actively test new models like GPT-5 for their internal tools. At the same time, they are working relentlessly to keep users locked into their own platform. They are aware the landscape is changing, and they are arriving highly prepared to compete.

7 key takeaway from @Worldpay_Global global payments report

1/ Digital wallets have won

They now account for 56% of global e-commerce and 33% of POS value in 2025. Alipay, Apple Pay, PayPal, and Mercado Pago collectively drove 38% of all consumer-to-business volume over $13.8 trillion. 

The story isn't just growth; wallets are becoming superapps (Alipay, WeChat Pay, GCash, Grab) that bundle banking, e-commerce, and messaging into one platform.

2/ Payment apps are taking over physical retail. 

Apps accounted for 37% of global POS value in 2025 and are forecast to hit 46% ($15.6T) by 2030, growing at 8% CAGR (135% faster than the POS channel overall). 

APAC led this shift, with China at 89% payment app POS share, India at 65%, and Thailand at 56%.

3/ QR codes are the global bridge. 

They dominate in APAC and LATAM: China 80%, Philippines 74%, Argentina 84%, Colombia 76%. 

In Europe and North America, QR adoption is muted due to entrenched card infrastructure, but that's changing.

4/ Glocalization is reshaping cross-border. 

Most payment systems were built for domestic use. Now Alipay, WeChat Pay, UPI, and Brazil's Pix are all expanding internationally through interoperability agreements and direct acceptance networks creating a multipolar payment world that competes with Visa/Mastercard rails for cross-border flows.

5/ BNPL evolved from a product to infrastructure. 

Global e-com BNPL grew 130x since 2014, reaching ~$300B in 2025, forecast to hit $500B by 2030 at 11% CAGR. 

But the bigger story is that BNPL brands like @Affirm , @AfterpayUSA , and @Klarna have morphed into full financial services platforms including issuing debit/credit cards, savings accounts, and stablecoins (Klarna issued KlarnaUSD). Meanwhile, traditional card issuers are fighting back with card-backed installments.

6/ Crypto: evolution, not revolution but stablecoins are the exception. 

Direct crypto payments remain niche at 0.19% of e-commerce payment volumes. 

Stablecoins are quietly becoming serious payments infrastructure with stablecoin-linked card spending hitting $4.5B in 2025, up 6x from 2024. @Visa issued crypto card spending rose 525% in 2025. 

7/ Cards are resilient, not dying. 

Despite wallet growth, cards represented 48% of POS and 31% of e-commerce value in 2025, accounting for $16.1T in direct spending. 

Card volumes continue rising globally, largely through wallets as the underlying funding mechanism.

MercadoLibre is the engine of the LATAM economy.

Think about everything they power — millions of small and medium businesses selling on their marketplace, consumers transacting across e-commerce, Mercado Pago processing payments and moving money across borders, Mercado Crédito extending credit to individuals and businesses that traditional banks have long ignored, and a logistics network quietly stitching it all together.

Now try to imagine LATAM without $MELI.

That’s the test. And when a business becomes that difficult to imagine away — when entire economies, merchants, and consumers are built around what it enables — that’s when you know you’re looking at something special.

This isn’t just an e-commerce company. 

It’s financial infrastructure. 

It’s economic enablement. 

It’s the backbone of a digital economy that is still in its early stages of development across a region of 650+ million people.

Marcos Galperin has built something that doesn’t just participate in LATAM’s growth — it facilitates it. And that distinction matters a lot when thinking about the long-term compounding potential of this business.

The region is digitizing. The tailwinds are secular. And $MELI is the engine driving it forward.
